nutrient_source.title: Strawberry Fruit Cheese Tart (Original) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Strawberry Fruit Cheese Tart (Original), cr_id G00010484, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: Cheesecake — analogous estimate; International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 (Atkinson et al.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Strawberry Fruit Cheese Tart (Original). GI estimated at 45 (low) based on cheesecake analogues reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) International Tables of GI Values. The low carbohydrate content (8.85g/100g) and high fat and protein content strongly attenuate glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 7.08) / 100 ≈ 3.2 (serving = 80g, carbohydrate = 8.85 × 0.80 = 7.08g).

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension must avoid this item; a single 80g serving delivers approximately 1023 mg sodium, representing over 68% of the 1500 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (8.85g/100g) but high saturated fat and zero dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate and high protein profile (16.09g/100g) supports short-term blood glucose stability, but the high saturated fat content (~13.5g/100g) and absence of dietary fibre may impair long-term insulin sensitivity; consume in small portions only and pair with fibre-rich foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13.5g/100g), critically high sodium (1279 mg/100g), and cholesterol (78 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high saturated fat, critically elevated sodium, and dietary cholesterol makes this item unsuitable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; regular consumption is strongly discouraged.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13.5g/100g) and critically high sodium (1279 mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at approximately 13.5g/100g far exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6), while critically high sodium worsens joint and tissue swelling; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this item.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Strawberry Fruit Cheese Tart (Original) is a chilled dessert tart sold in Singaporean bakeries and dessert shops, consisting of a baked shortcrust or pâte sucrée pastry shell filled with a rich cream cheese mixture and topped with fresh whole or halved strawberries. The filling is dense, creamy, and mildly tangy from the cream cheese, balanced by a buttery, crumbly pastry base. The fresh strawberry topping provides a bright, slightly tart contrast to the rich filling. Per 100g, the tart delivers 307 kcal, 16.09g protein, 23.06g fat (with an estimated 13.5g saturated fat from cream cheese and butter), 8.85g carbohydrate, and 6.24g sugar. Sodium is critically high at 1279 mg/100g, making this item unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Calcium is notably high at 1375 mg/100g, reflecting the cream cheese content. Dietary fibre is zero. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low) due to the dominant fat and protein content attenuating glucose absorption. Clinically, this item is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat load, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to absent dietary fibre and high saturated fat despite low carbohydrate content. It is a popular teatime and snack item in Singapore, often sold as individual portions in patisseries and supermarket bakery sections.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a strawberry fruit cheese tart?
Can diabetics eat strawberry cheese tart?
Is strawberry cheese tart high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010484) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical provenance. Saturated fat is estimated from known cream cheese and butter composition as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this single field is medium confidence. All other macros and micronutrients are high confidence from the HPB primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Strawberry Fruit Cheese Tart (Original) are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010484) as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g (13.5g/100g) is estimated from the known fatty acid composition of cream cheese and butter as primary ingredients, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference values for cream cheese (NDB 01017); this is an adapted estimate and not directly reported by HPB. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; this is naturally zero for a dessert of this composition. GI is an analogous estimate based on cheesecake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in any approved source.
